title: Running a Compliance Program
subtitle: From scattered rules to a working system

hook.metaphor: Knowing the rules of a sport doesn't make a club run well — you need coaches, training, someone tracking the rulebook, and a way to catch problems before match day. A compliance *program* is that whole machine: the standing system that keeps a whole organisation playing by the rules, not just one heroic person remembering them all.
hook.scenario: Two firms both 'care about compliance.' One has a named compliance officer, training, monitoring, and clear reporting to the board. The other relies on one overworked person and good intentions. A new regulation lands. Which firm absorbs it without a crisis?

learn: At the centre sits the **compliance function** — a team (led by a **Chief Compliance Officer**) that's independent enough to challenge the business. It owns the obligations register, advises on new rules, and reports honestly upward. Independence matters: compliance can't be overruled by the people it's meant to check.

The program runs on a familiar cycle: **policies** (written standards everyone follows) → **training** (so people actually know them) → **monitoring & testing** (checking controls really work, not just exist — the documented-vs-implemented trap again) → **reporting** (telling management and the board the truth, including the bad news).

It fits the **three lines of defence** (from Operational Risk): the business owns compliance day-to-day (1st line), the compliance function sets standards and challenges (2nd line), and internal audit independently checks the whole thing (3rd line). Compliance that lives only in a siloed team — with the business treating it as 'not my job' — is a program destined to fail.

And the whole machine is powered by **culture and tone at the top** (yes, again — it's the golden thread of GRC). The best program on paper fails if leaders signal that hitting targets beats following rules. A genuine 'culture of compliance' is what makes the firm in our scenario absorb a new regulation as routine, while the other lurches into crisis.
bridge: So at work, you can assess whether compliance is *real* by looking for the machine, not the intentions: *Is there an independent compliance function? Policies people actually know? Monitoring that tests effectiveness? Honest reporting to the board? And a culture where following the rules is genuinely valued?* A program with all five turns regulation from a recurring fire drill into business as usual.

interactions.title: The compliance machine
interactions.prompt: Match each part of a compliance program to its job.

pairs.right: Independent team that owns and challenges compliance
Written standards everyone is expected to follow
Making sure people actually know the rules
Checking controls truly work, not just exist
Telling the board the truth, including bad news

interactions.title: Which firm survives the new rule?
interactions.prompt: A new regulation lands. The well-run program firm vs the 'one person + good intentions' firm. What's the lesson?

options.label: Good intentions are enough — process is bureaucracy.
options.feedback: Intentions don't scale or survive turnover. When the one overworked person misses something, there's no system to catch it. That's how 'we care' becomes 'we got fined'.

options.label: A standing program (function, policies, training, monitoring, reporting) absorbs change calmly.
options.feedback: Exactly. A real program turns a new rule into a routine update of the obligations register — not a crisis. The machine, not the hero, is what makes compliance durable.

options.label: Just hire one more person and keep winging it.
options.feedback: Adding bodies to an unstructured approach doesn't fix the missing system. Without policies, monitoring, and reporting, you've just got more people winging it.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “A compliance program is the standing machine — independent function, policies, training, monitoring, honest reporting, and a real culture of compliance — that lets a whole organisation keep playing by the rules without heroics.”

checkpoint.q: Why must the compliance function be independent?
options: So it can challenge the business and not be overruled by the people it checks
checkpoint.explain: Independence lets compliance honestly challenge and report — it can't be a function the business simply overrides.

checkpoint.q: 'Monitoring & testing' in a compliance program is there to…
options: Check that controls actually work, not merely exist on paper
checkpoint.explain: It closes the documented-vs-implemented gap by verifying controls are effective in practice.

checkpoint.q: The golden thread running through every GRC theme is…
options: Culture and tone at the top
checkpoint.explain: Even a perfect program on paper fails without leadership genuinely valuing doing the right thing.
